macro_segments.title: Meat from a Dangling Limb: Separation, Definition as Food, and Transmission of Tumah

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Dangling Limbs and the Status of Ever Min HaChai vs. Basar Min HaChai
The Core Case: When Meat is Cut from a Dangling Limb—Order of Actions and Tumah
Rabbi Assi's Question to Rabbi Zeira: How Does Tumah Transfer When Cutting at an Unexposed Surface?
Rabbi Zeira's Response: Rabbi Abba bar Mamal's Attribution to Rabbi Meir's Position
Rabbi Assi's Further Objection: Hechsher Requirement and the Lack of Shechita Blood
Rava's Resolution: Invoking the Principle of Metamei Tumah Chamurah Agav Aviv
Rava bar Rav Chanan's Alternative: Things That Were or Will Become a Source of Tumah

macro_segments.title: The Doctrine of Ma'aseh Etz: Why Past or Future Tumah Status Doesn't Eliminate Hechsher Requirement

micro_segments.title: Rava's Objection: Identity Change and the Principle of Panim Chadashot
Abaye's Series of Cases: Hardened Dough Repurposed as a Seat
Abaye's Question: Why Doesn't Theoretical Future Tumah Status Eliminate Hechsher?
Response: Ma'aseh Etz—Food with a Different Identity Has Different Rules
Second Case: Offerings to Avodah Zarah and the Identity of a Gift Rather Than Food
Third Case: Food Integrated into Vessels (Chiburei Ochlin Ke-Keilim)
Fourth Case: Fat of a Neveila Connected to Kidneys (Shomer Principle)
Fifth Case: Seeds on a Roof Becoming Part of a Structure with Tzaraat

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Position on Dangling Limbs: The First Case (Living Animal)

micro_segments.title: Gemara's Initial Question: Which Case Is Rabbi Shimon Addressing?
Rabbi Yochanan's Explanation: Food Definition Requires Ability to Feed to Others
Rabbi Zeira's Counter: The Simpler Explanation of Physical Connection (Me'ura Be'Me'ura)
The Tree Limb Analogy: Fruit on a Dangling Branch Still Physically Attached

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Position on Dangling Limbs: The Middle Case (Shechted Animal)

micro_segments.title: The Debate: Does Shechita Blood Make a Dangling Limb Susceptible to Tumah?
Rabbi Yochanan's Explanation via Food Definition: Non-Consumability
Gemara's Rejection: Too Many Other Explanations Exist for Why Shechita Doesn't Apply

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Position on Dangling Limbs: The Final Case (Dead Animal—Dangling Meat)

micro_segments.title: The Case: Dangling Meat (Not a Whole Limb) When the Animal Dies
The Halacha: Dangling Meat Requires Hechsher but Cannot Be Eaten by Anyone
Rabbi Shimon's Response: Basar Min HaChai Cannot Be Called Food
Chachamim's Counter: Physical Ability to Eat, Not Halachic Permissibility, Defines Food

macro_segments.title: Human Dangling Limbs and Meat: Extension of Laws and Disputes to Humans

micro_segments.title: Dangling Limb and Meat of a Human: Both Are Tahor (No Tumah Source)
Upon Human Death: Ever Min HaChai Causes Tumah; Basar Min HaChai Does Not

macro_segments.title: Human Dangling Limbs and Meat: Extension of Laws and Disputes to Humans (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara's Question: How Can a Dangling Limb Be Tahor When the Person Dies?
Clarification: Rabbi Shimon Disputes the Status of Ever Min HaMeis, Not Ever Min HaChai
Rabbi Eliezer's Baraita: Ever Min HaChai Causes Tumah; Ever Min HaMeis Does Not (Disputed)
Rabbi Yehoshua's Kal Va-Chomer: Dead Body Is Tamei; Its Limb Should Be Tamei Even More
Rabbi Eliezer's Response: This Is What I Received as Tradition

macro_segments.title: Practical Distinctions: Ever Min HaChai vs. Ever Min HaMeis and Pieces Separated from Them

micro_segments.title: The Difference: Ever Min HaChai Requires Whole Limb; Ever Min HaMeis Counts Pieces
Dispute Over Flesh Separated from Ever Min HaChai: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua
Dispute Over Bone Separated from Ever Min HaChai: Rabbi Nechunya vs. Rabbi Yehoshua
Final Reconciliation: Rabbi Shimon's Tahor May Apply to Pieces, Not Whole Ever Min HaMeis

topical_tags.term: Ever Min HaChai (dangling limb from living being)
Basar Min HaChai (meat from living being)
Hechsher (susceptibility to tumah through contact with liquid)
Shechita (ritual slaughter) and blood of shechita
Metamei Tumah Chamurah Agav Aviv (heavier tumah source overrides hechsher requirement)
Rabbi Meir's position on non-exposed surface transmission
Rabbi Shimon (disputes dangling limb tumah status)
Food definition (ochel): requires ability to feed to others
Abaye (presents cases of theoretical future tumah)
Chiburei Ochlin Ke-Keilim (food integrated into vessels)
Rabbi Eliezer (position on human limbs and tumah)
Rabbi Yehoshua (disputes Rabbi Eliezer with kal vachomer)
Rabbi Nechunya ben HaKana (position on separated bone)
